TITLE: Exploring the status of artificial intelligence for healthcare research in Africa: a bibliometric and thematic analysis
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Published AI-for-health work concerning Africa is internationally distributed and concentrated in a limited set of countries and institutions.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Lens-indexed AI-for-health publications retrieved with an Africa-focused search.
KEY_FINDING: The study reported substantial participation by institutions outside Africa and a concentration of African output in South Africa.
LIMITATION: The broad keyword search and mixed publication types do not define the ML4Africa corpus used in the present study.

TITLE: Stuck in the middle: a systematic review of authorship in collaborative health research in Africa, 2014-2016
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: International collaboration can coexist with reduced local representation in prominent authorship positions.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 7100 PubMed-indexed health articles concerning sub-Saharan Africa published from 2014 through 2016.
KEY_FINDING: Local authors represented 52.9% of first authors overall, but representation was lower in papers involving collaborators from the United States, Canada, or Europe.
LIMITATION: Affiliation-based geographic classification and a historical corpus limit direct comparison with ML4Africa.

TITLE: Geographic, Subject, and Authorship Trends among LMIC-based Scientific Publications in High-impact Global Health and General Medicine Journals: A 30-Month Bibliometric Analysis
JOURNAL: Journal of Epidemiology and Global Health
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Authorship representation in high-impact global-health publishing varies by geography and institutional income setting.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: LMIC-based scientific publications in selected high-impact global-health and general-medicine journals over 30 months.
KEY_FINDING: The study documented geographic and authorship imbalances within a selected high-impact journal sample.
LIMITATION: Journal selection and short observation period limit generalization to a closed interdisciplinary corpus.

TITLE: Where there is no local author: a network bibliometric analysis of authorship parasitism among research conducted in sub-Saharan Africa
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Absence of any locally affiliated author has been documented in a substantial minority of research conducted in sub-Saharan Africa.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 32061 PubMed-indexed articles reporting research conducted in sub-Saharan Africa from 2014 through 2018.
DESIGN: Bibliometric review with network analysis and multivariable regression
KEY_FINDING: Fourteen point eight percent of articles had no author affiliated with the study country according to the study definition.
LIMITATION: The present study does not observe study sites and therefore must not assign this label from authorship order or affiliation composition alone.

TITLE: Bibliometric analysis of sub-Saharan African and US authorship in publications about sub-Saharan Africa funded by the Fogarty International Center, 2008-2020
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: First and last authorship are frequently used as imperfect indicators of scientific leadership and career visibility.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 3446 Fogarty International Center-funded publications concerning sub-Saharan Africa from 2008 through 2020.
KEY_FINDING: Sub-Saharan Africa-affiliated first and last authorship increased over time, while median relative citation ratios were modestly lower than for US-affiliated first or last authors.
LIMITATION: A funder-specific corpus and relative citation ratio are not directly equivalent to the ML4Africa corpus and OpenAlex H-index.

TITLE: Bibliometric Analysis of Authorship of Publications About Sub-Saharan Africa Published in the Journal Sexually Transmitted Diseases, 2011 to 2020
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Underrepresentation in first and last authorship positions has been reported even when African-affiliated authors are present elsewhere in the byline.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 148 articles about sub-Saharan Africa published in one specialty journal from 2011 through 2020.
KEY_FINDING: Thirty-six percent of first authors and 40% of last authors had a sub-Saharan African affiliation; 8.8% of articles had no such author.
LIMITATION: Single-journal scope limits external validity.

TITLE: Authorship inequalities in global health research: the IeDEA Southern Africa collaboration
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: A collaboration may include authors from participating countries while still showing unequal distribution of prominent authorship positions.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 313 IeDEA Southern Africa articles published from 2007 through 2020.
KEY_FINDING: Authors from low-income or lower-middle-income countries were less likely than high-income-country authors to occupy first or last positions, although representation increased over time.
LIMITATION: One established collaboration and World Bank income groups do not map directly to the Africa versus outside-Africa strata used here.

TITLE: Bibliometric analysis of authorship patterns in publications from a research group at the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, 2016-2020
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Affiliation patterns and journal prestige can be associated with representation in first and last authorship positions.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 882 papers involving one UK-based global-health research group and research conducted in 61 LMICs.
DESIGN: Bibliometric case study with multinomial regression
KEY_FINDING: LMIC-only affiliated authors were less likely than HIC-only affiliated authors to be first or last authors, especially in higher-impact journals and multicountry studies.
LIMITATION: Single-group case study and income-group comparisons limit direct transfer to an Africa-focused corpus.

TITLE: Authorship Distribution and Under-Representation of Sub-Saharan African Authors in Global Oncology Publications
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Sub-Saharan African representation may be greater in middle authorship than in first or last authorship.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 608 JCO Global Oncology articles published from October 2015 through March 2020.
KEY_FINDING: For studies about sub-Saharan Africa, 44.8% of first and 40.7% of last authors were regionally affiliated, compared with 68.8% of middle authors.
LIMITATION: Journal- and oncology-specific scope limits generalization.

TITLE: Authorship representation in global emergency medicine: a bibliometric analysis from 2016 to 2020
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Funding and country income context are associated with local representation in prominent authorship positions.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 1751 global emergency-medicine articles reporting studies in LMICs from 2016 through 2020.
KEY_FINDING: High-income-country authors were the largest affiliation group, and study-country funding was associated with greater odds of a local first author.
LIMITATION: Emergency-medicine and income-group definitions differ from the present corpus and regional strata.

TITLE: Defining the Role of Authors and Contributors
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Byline position is not a complete measure of contribution because authorship entails contribution, manuscript review, approval, and accountability, while order conventions vary.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Biomedical journal authorship and contributor reporting.
KEY_FINDING: ICMJE specifies four authorship criteria and notes that author-order conventions are determined by author groups.
LIMITATION: Guidance does not provide an empirical mapping from byline position to leadership.

TITLE: OpenAlex: A fully-open index of scholarly works, authors, venues, institutions, and concepts
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: OpenAlex provides openly accessible linked metadata for scholarly works, authors, institutions, and related entities.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Global scholarly metadata graph.
KEY_FINDING: OpenAlex exposes its data through a web interface, downloadable snapshot, and REST API.
LIMITATION: Coverage and author or institution disambiguation are imperfect and evolve over time.

TITLE: About PubMed
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: PubMed is a freely accessible biomedical and life-sciences citation and abstract resource maintained by NCBI at the US National Library of Medicine.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Biomedical and life-sciences literature indexed by PubMed.
KEY_FINDING: PubMed aggregates MEDLINE, PubMed Central citation records, and Bookshelf records for literature discovery and metadata retrieval.
LIMITATION: Not every scientific publication is indexed and full text is generally hosted elsewhere.

TITLE: S2ORC: The Semantic Scholar Open Research Corpus
JOURNAL: Proceedings of the 58th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Semantic Scholar-derived resources provide structured scholarly metadata, references, and text-mining-oriented records.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Multidisciplinary scholarly publications aggregated into S2ORC.
KEY_FINDING: S2ORC unified metadata and resolved bibliographic references across publishers and archives.
LIMITATION: The 2020 corpus description is not a guarantee of current API completeness for every DOI.

TITLE: ORCID and Persistent Identifiers
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: ORCID iDs provide persistent person identifiers that can support cross-source author reconciliation.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Researchers and scholarly records linked through ORCID.
KEY_FINDING: ORCID describes the iD as a persistent identifier for a person and supports connections with work identifiers such as DOI.
LIMITATION: ORCID records may be incomplete, private, unauthenticated in downstream metadata, or absent.

TITLE: An index to quantify an individual's scientific research output
JOURNAL: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: The H-index combines publication volume and citation counts into a single author-level indicator.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Physicists and general researcher-level bibliometric assessment.
KEY_FINDING: The H-index was proposed as a compact measure of cumulative research output and citation impact.
LIMITATION: It is career-length, field, database, and authorship-context dependent.

TITLE: The h-index: Advantages, limitations and its relation with other bibliometric indicators at the micro level
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: H-index should not be treated as a context-free or standalone measure of scientific quality.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Researcher-level bibliometric indicators.
KEY_FINDING: H-index was strongly related to publication and citation quantity and added limited independent information when used alone.
LIMITATION: Results arise from the studied population and bibliometric database.

TITLE: Inferring gender from first names: Comparing the accuracy of Genderize, Gender API, and the gender R package on authors of diverse nationality
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Name-based gender inference can have high aggregate accuracy yet vary by naming context and cannot represent self-identified or nonbinary gender.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Clinical trial authors with manually labelled gender across diverse national contexts.
KEY_FINDING: Genderize and Gender API exceeded 95% overall accuracy in the evaluated dataset, with lower performance for several East and Southeast Asian name groups and complex names.
LIMITATION: Gold-standard labels and binary tool outputs do not establish self-identified gender; performance is population dependent.

TITLE: Performance of gender detection tools: a comparative study of name-to-gender inference services
JOURNAL: Journal of the Medical Library Association
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Different name-to-gender services yield materially different accuracy and coverage, supporting cautious exploratory use rather than individual-level classification.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: 6131 first names with known labels assessed across multiple inference services.
KEY_FINDING: Tool performance varied substantially, and Genderize was less accurate than the highest-performing service in the evaluated sample.
LIMITATION: Binary labels, name origin, diacritics, and sample composition affect validity.

TITLE: The Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) statement: guidelines for reporting observational studies
CLAIM_SUPPORTED: Transparent reporting requires explicit design, variables, data sources, bias, statistical methods, and limitations.
POPULATION_OR_SCOPE: Cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional observational studies.
KEY_FINDING: STROBE provides a checklist for complete reporting of observational research.
LIMITATION: It is a reporting framework rather than a quality score and was not designed specifically for bibliometric studies.
